input.prompt: I have an existing xUnit test project at ./test-output/MyTests/MyTests.csproj. Create a reusable template from it so my team can use it for new test projects. The template should be called 'Team Test Project'.

data.content: I have an existing xUnit test project at ./test-output/MyTests/MyTests.csproj. Create a reusable template from it so my team can use it for new test projects. The template should be called 'Team Test Project'.
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-14T00:56:09.384Z</current_datetime>

I have an existing xUnit test project at ./test-output/MyTests/MyTests.csproj. Create a reusable template from it so my team can use it for new test projects. The template should be called 'Team Test Project'.

input.initialPrompt: I have an existing xUnit test project at ./test-output/MyTests/MyTests.csproj. Create a reusable template from it so my team can use it for new test projects. The template should be called 'Team Test Project'.

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to create a .NET template from their existing xUnit test project. I need to:

1. First, look at the existing project to understand its structure
2. Create a `.template.config` folder with a `template.json` file that defines the template

Let me start by exploring the existing project structure.

data.content: Now I'll create the `.template.config/template.json` file to make this a reusable .NET template:

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-9c1a719c6a734604932215b22999551a/test-output/MyTests/.template.config/template.json b/tmp/sv-9c1a719c6a734604932215b22999551a/test-output/MyTests/.template.config/template.json
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-9c1a719c6a734604932215b22999551a/test-output/MyTests/.template.config/template.json
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+{
+  "$schema": "http://json.schemastore.org/template",
+  "author": "Team",
+  "classifications": ["Test", "xUnit"],
+  "identity": "TeamTestProject",
+  "name": "Team Test Project",
+  "shortName": "team-test",
+  "tags": {
+    "language": "C#",
+    "type": "project"
+  },
+  "sourceName": "MyTests",
+  "preferNameDirectory": true
+}
+

data.content: Now let's verify the template is valid by installing it:

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-9c1a719c6a734604932215b22999551a/test-output/MyTests && dotnet new install . 2>&1 | head -20", "description": "Install and verify the template"}

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net9.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.11.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit" Version="2.9.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.2">
      <IncludeAssets>runtime; build; native; contentfiles; analyzers; buildtransitive</IncludeAssets>
      <PrivateAssets>all</PrivateAssets>
    </PackageReference>
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

=== UnitTest1.cs ===
using Xunit;

namespace SampleTests;
public class UnitTest1
{
    [Fact]
    public void Test1() { }
}

data.content:
The template works perfectly. The namespace was correctly replaced from `MyTests` to `SampleTests` based on the project name.

**Done!** I created a `.template.config/template.json` in your project at `./test-output/MyTests/.template.config/`. The template:

- **Name:** Team Test Project
- **Short name:** `team-test`
- **Usage:** `dotnet new team-test -n YourProjectName`

The `sourceName` setting ensures the `MyTests` namespace/filename is automatically replaced with whatever name the user provides via `-n`.
